data.title: Weatherford International: Too Cheap (Again) To Ignore
data.description:
Executive Summary

Weatherford International (NASDAQ:WFRD) has improved tremendously since emerging from bankruptcy at the end of 2019 and the company is in a strong position to weather (no pun intended) a potential economic slowdown. The balance sheet is solid, and the company is at the forefront of technological innovations to help reduce customers’ costs by increasing efficiencies. The focus on solutions businesses has improved margins significantly and are now comparable and even higher than peers. The stock has traded down significantly in the last year and the valuation is extremely low. The risk/reward seems to be tilted to the upside despite a tougher oil price environment. I initiate with a Strong Buy and a target of $100/share.

Background

Weatherford International (WFRD) in its current form was formed through bankruptcy in 2019. The company emerged from bankruptcy on December 16, 2019, after shedding most of its debt, $6.2 billion, to be exact, and with a new board. The former bondholders became the owners, and the ownership became more concentrated. The company hired Girish Saligram in September 2020. The management's focus has been on solutions businesses rather than hard equipment assets. They also shed a few businesses before filing for bankruptcy, like its oil-well business in 2017 and the Laboratory Services Business in 2019.

The stock was $1/share in the summer of 2020 and rallied to above $130/share in 2024. The stock is now around $45/share and market capitalization of $3.2 billion.

Ownership and Management

Diluted shares outstanding are 74.5 million. The two largest shareholders are Vanguard and BlackRock with 7.7 million shares and 7.3 million shares respectively, which is 10.3% and 9.8% holdings. The third largest is Capital Research Global Investors at 5.9 million/7.9%, followed by Price T Rowe Associates and T. Rowe Price Investment Management at 5.7 million shares and 4.4 million shares respectively, or 7.6% and 5.9%. Those are the shareholders above 5% holdings. No significant insider ownership or recent activity.

The company's C-suite changed with the balance sheet restructuring in 2019. As mentioned, the CEO, Girish Saligram, was hired in 2020. He came from Exterran Corp. as their COO, which he had been since 2016. Before then, he was in various roles at GE for 20 years. The CFO is Arun Mitra. He joined Weatherford in 2023 with more than 30 years of experience across multiple industries. The board of directors consists of six directors and the CEO is on the board. The board is led by Charles M. (Chuck) Sledge. Among other roles, he serves on the board of Talos Energy, which is another public oil and gas company.

Business Summary

The company’s business has consistently improved since emerging from bankruptcy. Revenue was $3.7 billion in 2020 and $5.5 billion in 2024 and Operating Income improved from negative $44 million to $938 million. EBITDA improved from $459 million to $1281 million, and margins went from 12.5% to 23.2%. Free cash flow for, 2024 was $524mm. The business improvement has led to a better balance sheet as well; debt coming out of bankruptcy was reduced to $2.5 billion from above $8 billion, and is now down to $1.6 billion. The company is now in a good position regarding both its business and its balance sheet.

The company is guiding for $5.1 to $5.35 billion in revenues and $1.2-$1.35 billion in EBITDA for 2025. I forecast ~$5 billion in revenues and $900 million in EBITDA due to lower oil prices and tariff issues since the company put out guidance. The lower oil price will most likely lead to lower activity by oil and gas producers, which are the customers.

Weatherford International is an oil service company that operates through three segments: Drilling and Evaluation, Well Construction and Completions, and Production and Intervention. The company is headquartered in Houston, Texas. The company operates in 75 countries and has 330 operating locations and is one of the largest oil services companies in the world after Schlumberger (SLB), Halliburton (HAL), and Baker Hughes (BKR). The company’s revenues in 2024 from the US were 15%, Mexico 12% (with 10% from one customer, presumably Pemex), Saudi Arabia 10%, and no other country or customer over 10%. Looking at regions, North America is 19%, Latin America 25%, Europe/Sub-Saharan Africa and Russia 17% and Middle-East/North Africa and Asia 38%. The Middle-East segment has grown from 32% in 2022 to now 38% and the US has stagnated from 25% to now 19% of total revenue.

The company has significant exposure to a ramp-up or slowdown in OPEC activity. The company mentioned on its last earnings call that they have seen a slowdown in Russia and in Latin America, which was a big reason to guide for lower revenue in 2025.

The company is investing in R&D, as it is key to have a strong technology offering as oil and gas producers constantly seek to improve and optimize their production. The company’s products and services are designed to enable clients (oil and gas producers/E&P companies) to increase production rates while reducing their costs of drilling and production. All the company’s segments, according to the 10K, “are enabled by a suite of digital monitoring, control, and optimization solutions using advanced analytics to provide safe, reliable and efficient solutions throughout the well life cycle, including responsible abandonment at the end of the well’s productive life.”

The Drilling and Evaluation segment offers various services like managed pressure drilling, drilling services, wireline, and drilling fluids. The company’s services are focused on optimizing reserves and productivity of the oil and gas wells. The segments' revenue was $1682 million in 2024 or 30.5% of total sales and EBITDA was $467 million or 34.6% of total EBITDA with an EBITDA margin of 27.8%.

The Well Construction and Completions segment offers products and services throughout the life of the well. The offerings are tubular running services, cementation products, completions, liner hangers, and well services. The company deploys conventional to advanced technologies to keep the process safe and efficient during the well construction phase. The segments' revenue was $1976 million in 2024 or 36% of sales and EBITDA was $564 million or 41.8% of total EBITDA with an EBITDA margin of 28.7%.

The Production and Intervention segment offers several reservoir stimulation designs and engineering capabilities to unlock reserves in deep water, old reservoirs, and also in conventional and unconventional wells. The offerings are intervention services and drilling tools, artificial lift, digital solutions, sub-sea intervention, and pressure pumping in select markets. The segments' revenue was $1452 million in 2024 or 26.3% of total sales and EBITDA was $329 million or 24.4% of total EBITDA with an EBITDA margin of 22%.

The company also had $403 million in 2024 of Other Sales with EBITDA of $84 million or 6.2% of total EBITDA with an EBITDA margin of 20.8%.

The company invests in R&D over $100 million per year. These are some of the technological achievements the company highlighted in the last earnings press release.

“Technology Highlights

On January 14, 2025, at the annual IKTVA forum held at Dahan Dharan Expo, Weatherford signed an agreement with SPARK, a fully integrated industrial ecosystem aimed at making Saudi Arabia a global energy hub. This strategic partnership, aligned with Saudi Arabia’s Vision 2030, enhances Weatherford’s local presence, boosts production capabilities, and supports the region’s energy goals. By advancing local content, fostering talent, and driving innovation, Weatherford demonstrates its commitment to economic growth and to supporting Saudi Arabia’s leadership in energy innovation.

Drilling & Evaluation (DRE)

In the North Sea, Weatherford successfully deployed the world’s first Dual Advanced Kickover Tool for Equinor. The unique solution enables gas lift valve replacements in just a single run, which significantly increases efficiency and reduces the cost of conventional systems.

In Saudi Arabia, Weatherford deployed its compact wireline logging tools with shuttle technology to achieve a record total depth for Aramco. This extended reach well features the longest horizontal section, measuring 23,000 feet.

Well Construction and Completions (WCC)

In deepwater Brazil, Weatherford successfully installed the first OptiRoss® RFID Multi-Cycle Sliding Sleeve Valve for a major operator. The system enhances acid stimulation efficiency, improving production and boosting the reservoir’s oil recovery factor.

In the Middle East, Weatherford successfully deployed its market-leading Optimax Tubing Retrievable Safety Valve for an NOC. This deployment enabled gas lift valve replacements in a single run, significantly increasing efficiency and reducing costs compared to conventional systems.

Production and Intervention (PRI)

In the Middle East, Weatherford's Alpha1Go remote re-entry system was deployed for an NOC, optimizing rig site operations by significantly reducing whipstock preparation time and minimizing red-zone exposure. This deployment improved both efficiency and safety, demonstrating the system's effectiveness in facilitating well re-entry operations and real-time team collaboration in various rig environments.

In US land operations, Weatherford successfully deployed its first Reclaim Dual Barrier Plug and Abandon (P&A) system for a major operator. This innovative dual-barrier P&A system safely and reliably abandons wells without the need to pull tubing. By eliminating the requirement for conventional drilling rigs, it significantly reduces costs and minimizes the carbon footprint.”

The balance sheet underwent a restructuring in 2019 and the company cut $6.2 billion in debt. The company has since reduced it from $2.5 billion in 2020 to $1.6 billion in 2024. The free cash flow for 2024 was over $500 million and over $600 million in 2023. The net debt calculation is only 0.5x and the debt is rated just below investment grade at BB- and a positive outlook by S&P Global.

The company recently launched a buyback program of $500 million over the next three years and initiated a 25c quarterly dividend. In the second half of 2024, the company bought back $100 million of stock and looking to buy back $200 million in 2025. The SBC (share-based compensation) program has around 1.5 million shares per year. Buying back $200 million of shares around $40/share would be 5 million shares for a net of 3.5 million share reduction in 2025.

Value Proposition

Weatherford is clearly a stronger company than 5 years ago, with revenue up close to 60%. The company’s margins have also improved significantly, and the company is in the mid-20% EBITDA margins, which are comparable and even higher vs. public peers. The fact that the company has become a high-margin business seems overlooked by the market. The management even indicated they are still on track for high 20s EBITDA margins in a few years. I believe that it might be harder to achieve in a tougher oil price environment, but longer term it should be doable. The company’s focus on value-added technology has really helped with pricing power. I have EBITDA going lower for 2025 but rebounding in 2026. The company will continue to generate strong free cash flow, and the balance sheet will continue to be solid. The lower valuation vs. peers is not justified in my view, and the overall lower industry valuation could change as soon as later this year as the market looks forward through the tariff debacle.

The growth has been strong but might slow down a bit going forward, but even if 2025 is lower, I have topline rebounding to 5-10% going forward. The company grew 18-19% in 2022 and 2023 and slowed down to 7% in 2024.

The multiple historically for the peers has been above 10x EBITDA vs. 6-7x for (WFRD). (HAL) is currently trading at 5x and the 5-year average is 11x; (SLB) is trading at 7x and the 5-year average is 12x; and (BKR) is trading at 9x and the 5-year average is 10x. The most likely reason for (BKR) trading better is its exposure to LNG terminals.

I believe 8x is fair for a globally diversified oil service company with EBITDA margins in the mid-20% and strong free cash flow. EBITDA for 2024 was $1.3 billion and my estimates for 2025 and 2026 are $900 million and $1050 million, respectively. The stock target is therefore $100/share. See below. The stock is currently trading at 3.1x ltm EBITDA and 4.2x 2025 estimated EBITDA and back down to 3.2x 2026 estimated EBITDA.

Catalysts

Continued improved business performance and stock buyback program. The US business, which has lagged behind the international, might improve under the Trump administration's “drill baby drill” slogan. Also, OPEC is telegraphing more production going forward and as the company has close to 40% of revenue from the region, they might benefit from it. The stock might re-rate IF there is no global meltdown from the US tariff regime.

Risks

The biggest risk is the cyclical nature of oil prices and the industry activity around it. OPEC activity is slowing down. The US onshore shale business continues to experience slow activity. A global recession due to the US tariff regime.

Conclusion

The company has improved its business, reduced its balance sheet risk and the company is much different looking vs. pre-bankruptcy. The stock has recently traded down significantly and the valuation is extremely low. The risk/reward despite a tougher environment seems skewed to the upside.

data.title: SLR Investment: Strong 11% Dividend, Weak Overall Performance
data.description:
Investment Thesis

SLR Investment Corp. (Nasdaq: NASDAQ:SLRC) is a highly defensive investment choice that primarily attracts income- and dividend-oriented investors.

The Company is an externally managed BDC, with investment activities overseen by SLR Capital Partners, and has one of the highest-quality portfolios in the sector. However, despite its solid balance sheet, investors appear indifferent.

Although it offers a strong dividend yield of just under 11% (4 × $0.41 for 2024), which for now appears secure, the stock’s weak performance justifies a “HOLD” recommendation, mainly on the grounds that there are more compelling alternatives with similar characteristics.

In fact, SLRC may not even qualify for inclusion in a portfolio on diversification grounds alone.

Stock Overview

A key factor that prevents me from easily considering the stock a buy is its weak share price performance.

Surprisingly, despite posting solid financial metrics, SLRC is not among the market’s “favorite” BDCs, something clearly reflected in its lukewarm 5-year return of just +6%. Even when including dividends, the total return still fails to surpass +75%.

The underperformance of the stock becomes apparent, especially when compared to the total return of the VanEck BDC Income ETF (BIZD), which provides a general benchmark for the broader BDC universe, as well as for certain individual BDCs that I view as top-tier picks, such as Ares Capital Corporation (ARCC), Main Street Capital Corporation (MAIN) and Hercules Capital (HTGC).

The half of the return generated by SLRC compared to BIZD makes investors avoid selecting this stock even for portfolio diversification focused on income generation. If the goal was exposure, without emphasizing maximum total return, an investor would prefer BIZD.

To be clear, I am not suggesting that SLRC’s performance is negative, when evaluating its high-quality portfolio and skilled management team (which we’ll examine below), but the stock fails to differentiate itself from peer companies in the sector. I suspect that the company's NAV dynamics might explain this unusual market behavior.

The recent Trump tariff announcements, together with market pressure, caused SLRC stock to decline by 19% from its 2025 peak. The stock reached its three-year peak at a level that stood 32% lower than its previous decade-high point. The current stock price of $15 represents a 100% increase from pandemic lows.

The stock shows a prolonged declining pattern until a strong upward breakout above the three-year peak will start to change investor perceptions about this stock.

A Look at the NAV

As mentioned earlier, SLRC’s NAV picture is a little bizarre. Despite net assets increasing to $992.9 million or $18.20 per share in 2024, this represents only a 0.6% annual growth rate, and the more concerning is that expectations for a meaningful improvement from this stagnant trend remain quite low.

To be more precise, SLRC’s NAV was unchanging in the past decade, with a total growth of just 7% over the last 10 years. While this might suggest a very stable and resilient structure, it also leaves little room for upside potential.

To put this into perspective: ARCC has achieved a NAV increase of +153%, MAIN +156%, and HTGC +168% over the same period.

Therefore, no matter how high the quality of SLRC’s portfolio may be, its weak track record in terms of growth may prevent long-term investors, while combined with the stock’s gradual downward drift, the generous almost 11% dividend ($0.41 for Q4) simply isn’t enough to compensate.

At current levels, SLRC is trading at a 17% discount to NAV, almost 0.83x, placing it close to the sector-wide average for BDCs.

On the other hand, the “high-quality” names remain at a premium despite ongoing selling pressure: MAIN trades at a 70% premium, HTGC at 50%, and TRIN at 8%.

Incidentally, ARCC is trading at par (1:1), which could present a compelling entry point—but that’s a separate discussion beyond the scope of this article.

One could argue that discounted names like SLRC represent attractive opportunities. My view, however, is that such stocks are priced low or lower for a reason, just as the better-positioned names naturally deserve the premiums they trade at.

Portfolio and Financial Profile

SLR’s portfolio is extremely defensive, with more than 96% of total investments allocated to first-lien loans.

A more detailed analysis shows that about 70% of the portfolio is backed by assets and equipment finance (any tangible collateral other than real estate) which provides additional value and security to the underlying loans because of the quality and recoverability of the pledged assets.

The financial metrics are solid. Net investment income-NII remained stable at $0.44 for Q4 (and $1.77 for the full year 2024), ensuring the $0.41 quarterly dividend.

Even more interesting is SLRC’s relatively low leverage, with a debt-to-equity ratio of 1.03x (well below the BDC sector average of 1.18x).

For context, several peers have even lower leverage levels:

Gladstone Capital Corporation (GLAD) at 0.66x, Fidus Investment Corp. (FDUS) at 0.73x, Main Street Capital Corporation (MAIN) at 0.76x, and Hercules Capital (HTGC) at 0.89x. Ares Capital Corporation (ARCC) is almost identical at 1.03x, and Trinity Capital (TRIN) stands slightly higher at 1.06x.

In general, the closer a BDC gets to the statutory limit of 2.0x leverage, the less attractive it becomes. This is because higher leverage reduces flexibility for further borrowing and increases the company’s sensitivity to adverse conditions, whether from portfolio-specific stress events or broader market disruptions.

Conclusion

From all the above, it becomes clear that SLRC’s biggest weakness lies in its low growth expectations.

In broad terms, there’s not much, at least theoretically, that it lacks compared to the "best-in-class" BDCs, and frankly, it’s hard to imagine an investor actually losing money on this type of position.

In a less competitive field, SLRC would likely stand out as an excellent option in its own right. That’s precisely why I maintain a hold recommendation.

The dividend is secure and quite generous (just under 11%) and the quality of the portfolio helps shield the company from unfortunate events that could otherwise cause harm.

Unfortunately, it seems investors are not as drawn to the management’s conservative approach, as it reduces the likelihood of outsized gains through capital appreciation.

data.title: Stock Market Swoon Could Hit Some P&C Insurers Harder Than Others
data.description:
The investment portfolios of US property and casualty insurers currently stand to take a hit from the recent global sell-off in equities, but the magnitude of the impact on the vast majority of the companies in the sector is significantly inflated by one group's uniquely large allocation to common stocks.

Holdings in unaffiliated publicly traded common stocks among US P&C insurers hit $440.35 billion as of year-end 2024, according to an S&P Global Market Intelligence analysis of disclosures on Schedule D, Part 2, Section 2 of annual statutory filings, an amount that accounted for 17.7% of net admitted cash and invested assets.

The majority of the industry's exposure to publicly traded stocks sits within the subsidiaries of a single group, however, as the P&C subsidiaries of Berkshire Hathaway Inc. (BRK.B) reported holding $249.61 billion. While that value declined substantially from $323.13 billion a year earlier, reflecting the company's much-discussed shift to cash and short-term investments, it still was more than twice as large as any other US P&C group's position. Berkshire and the group led by State Farm Mutual Automobile Insurance Co. had 47.6% and 39.6% of their net admitted cash and invested assets allocated to unaffiliated publicly traded common equities. The rest of the industry's combined position amounted to just 5.1% of cash and invested assets, though there were several outliers with outsized concentrations in common equities.

If we were to hypothetically assume a drop in valuations in the holdings of P&C insurers excluding Berkshire equivalent to the S&P 500 Index's year-to-date decline of 13.2% through the April 7 market close, this would suggest a reduction in aggregate policyholders' surplus through a corresponding negative change in unrealized capital gains and losses of approximately $26.61 billion, or about 2.4% of the industry's Dec. 31, 2024, total.

With much of the market sell-off transpiring after the start of the second quarter, it will be months before any effects would manifest themselves in the form of hits to insurer balance sheets and capital-and-surplus accounts. But the underlying factors behind the market woes, including an elevated potential for an economic downturn, fears of significant inflation in consumer and producer prices, and diminished prospects for earnings-per-share growth, may present more of a clear and present threat to the industry.

Most P&C insurers traditionally concentrate a majority of their investments in investment-grade corporate bonds with significant allocations to securities issued by government agencies and the US federal government. Combined, those three asset classes accounted for nearly 52.9% of the industry's net admitted cash and invested assets at year-end 2024 when excluding Berkshire. Of the 1,132 US P&C groups and stand-alone P&C entities with data available as of April 7, there were 616 that showed no investment in unaffiliated, publicly traded common stocks whatsoever as of Dec. 31, 2024. Another 301 held some investments in publicly traded equities but at an amount that constituted less than 10% of their net admitted cash and invested assets.

We examined the top 25 unaffiliated, publicly traded common stocks held by P&C insurers other than Berkshire as of year-end 2024 by both the aggregate carrying value of the industry's positions and the number of P&C groups and stand-alone entities that held individual stocks. In both lists, the number of stocks that declined on a year-to-date basis, not surprisingly, far exceeded the count of those that appreciated in value though performances varied widely.

Shares of Eli Lilly and Co. (LLY), the industry's largest investment in an unaffiliated, publicly traded common stock by carrying value, fell by nearly 6.3% through the April 7 close, outperforming the S&P 500. But shares of Microsoft Corp. (MSFT), the industry's most widely owned name by number of holders, tumbled by 15.1% over the same timeframe. Apple Inc.'s (AAPL) shares, which would have easily topped the list of industry holdings by carrying value had Berkshire's mammoth position been incorporated in our analysis, plunged by 27.5% on a year-to-date basis through April 7.

The top-performing stock on each of the top lists was Abbott Laboratories, though its year-to-date gain of 10.4% through the April 7 close had marked a significant retreat from an early March peak. The industry's largest Abbott Labs (ABT) investors as of Dec. 31, 2024, were State Farm and Cincinnati Financial Corp.

At the other end of the spectrum, Broadcom Inc.'s (AVGO) 33.5% year-to-date decline was the largest among stocks with the broadest and deepest US P&C industry investment. State Farm along with the group led by Factory Mutual Insurance Co. and The Cincinnati held the largest positions by carrying value in the semiconductor developer's stock. Common stocks accounted for 46.7% and30.5%, respectively, of the year-end 2024 unaffiliated investments held by FM Global, the name by which Factory Mutual conducts business, and The Cincinnati. Other notable P&C insurers overweight equities relative to the industry as a whole include Auto Club Exchange Group, Markel Group Inc. (MKL) and the respective groups led by Palisades Safety & Insurance Association and Merrimack Mutual Fire Insurance Co.

The latter of those entities, which operate in combination with an affiliate and subsidiary as the Andover Companies, said in its 2024 annual statement that it has focused its portfolio on dividend-paying common stocks due to their track record of providing "significant cash flow over the last 10 years in a historically low interest rate environment." The company added that it maintains a "high quality" bond portfolio and conservative cash balances to avoid being put in a position where it might have to unload common stock during "inopportune periods" when equity prices are under pressure.

P&C insurers held relatively low levels of exposure to the S&P 500 constituents with the largest month-to-date share price declines through April 7. Among the 17 issues with drops of 20% or more in the first seven days of April, the combined Dec. 31, 2024, carrying value of the industry's holdings was less than $71.4 million. Positions in Zebra Technologies Corp. (ZBRA), which has plunged by 42.1% for the year and 20.9% for the month to date, accounted for $18.9 million of that tally.

These prospectively temporary declines in valuations are unlikely to have a significant impact on insurance company holders with large investment portfolios that are diversified across asset classes and strategies. But there are several smaller P&C insurers with outsized exposure to common equities.

Merchants Property Insurance Co. of Indiana, whose investments in unaffiliated, publicly traded common equities amounted to 102.3% of its year-end 2023 policyholders' surplus, might have the most interesting perspective on the situation.

A former homeowners and commercial package policy insurer that has been in run-off for more than 20 years, Indianapolis-based Merchants Property Insurance held 94 publicly traded common equities, including four positions that date back to 1937 and nine others that it originally acquired between 1949 and 1953. The latter investments, the company said in its 2024 annual statement, had been purchased as a hedge against post-World War II inflation. The strategy, it added, "has proven rewarding in the ensuing decades."

While Merchants Property Insurance indicated that it had taken some capital gains on its common stock positions in 2024 to "take advantage of a low tax climate," if we were to assume that its share counts in all 94 equities held steady on a year-to-date basis through April 7, the corresponding fair value decline would amount to an estimated $10.9 million, or 8.7% from the year-end total.

For most P&C insurers, the recent market declines could make a small dent in relatively large unrealized gain positions they had amassed. The aggregate carrying value of unaffiliated publicly traded common shares held by P&C insurers excluding Berkshire exceeded actual cost by $125.57 billion as of Dec. 31, 2024, up from $114.09 billion a year earlier. Of the groups and stand-alone entities that held unaffiliated, publicly traded common stocks as of year-end 2024, 85.6% showed carrying values that were higher than the actual cost of those investments.

The P&C industry, with and without Berkshire, produced a new high in realized capital gains on investments in unaffiliated common stock of $106.05 billion and $11.82 billion, respectively. In theory, this could provide dry powder for insurers to take advantage of buying opportunities left in the wake of the recent sell-off, though in some cases, more than a quarter of the way into 2025, the beneficiaries have already reinvested those proceeds elsewhere.

Our forthcoming 2025 US Insurance Investments Market Report will provide deeper insights into results and trends across asset classes across both the P&C and life sectors.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: DocuSign Delivers Growth Acceleration Through IAM - Reiterate Buy
data.description:
DOCU Is An Even Better Buy After The Selloff, Thanks To The Renewed Growth Opportunity Through IAM

We previously covered DocuSign, Inc. (NASDAQ:DOCU) in April 2024, discussing how it had remained laser-focused on generating new growth opportunities, recently through the Lexion acquisition and the new Intelligent Agreement Management (IAM) platform announced in April 2024.

Combined with the raised forward guidance, expanding Dollar Net Retention rate, healthy balance sheet, and attractive valuations, we reiterated our Buy rating then.

DOCU 1Y Stock Price

Since then, DOCU has had an impressive +81.3% stock price rally at its peak, before rapidly moderating upon the bursting AI bubble and the market-wide sell-off from mid-February 2025 onward – with the same correction also observed in its enterprise SaaS peers in varying degrees.

This is especially worsened by the market analysts projecting higher recessionary risks of up to 79% (compared to the prior estimates of 40%), with it potentially triggering the SaaS sector's elongated sales cycle and impacting top/bottom-line/backlog growth profile, DOCU included.

DOCU's Improving Financial & Performance Metrics

These developments may potentially derail the promising progress reported by DOCU thus far, as observed in the accelerating FQ4'25 subscription revenue growth at +9% YoY, compared to a quarter ago at +8% YoY and a year ago at +8% YoY.

Demand for its SaaS offerings remains robust as well, as observed in the still rich subscription gross margins of 84.1% (-0.2 points QoQ/-0.8 YoY/+0.1 from FY2020 levels of 84%).

This is on top of DOCU's richer adj operating margins of 29% (-1 points QoQ/+4 YoY) and growing Dollar Net Retention rate of 101% (+1 points QoQ/+3 YoY), with it underscoring the acceleration of the SaaS company's growth profile.

This is after the painful single-digit top-line expansions observed in the trough FY2025/FY2024 and the high double-digit growth observed between FY2019 and FY2022.

Much of DOCU's recovery tailwinds are naturally attributed to the launch of DocuSign IAM in May 2024, with the platform consolidating all of its customer's agreements/processes while improving efficiency and optimizing contracting cycles by up to 75%.

This new platform has already triggered renewed growth opportunity indeed, with it representing "over 20% of direct new customer deals" while being "the fastest-growing new product in DocuSign’s history" – perhaps underscoring why the SaaS company has been reporting robust billing growth by +22.7% QoQ in FQ4'25, albeit partly attributed to higher early renewals and more deals shifting to annual billing terms.

Combined with the sustained adoption of its existing offerings with nearly 1.7M customers (+10% YoY), we can understand why DOCU has felt confident to offer a promising guidance of IAM "representing a low double-digit percentage share of our total subscription recurring revenue book of business by Q4 of fiscal 2026," with the new platform already driving revenue growth in the US, the EU, and Latam on a QoQ basis.

These reasons are also why we believe that DOCU is likely to be well insulated through the robust enterprise demand.

This is despite the ongoing DOGE headwinds on current/future government contracts since the management has confirmed that it "don't have a big federal business today. It's really pretty modest."

With the new US administration yet to impose any tariffs on the SaaS sector, we believe that DOCU may continue to sustain the growth acceleration moving forward, as observed in the growing multi-year remaining performance obligations of $2.4B in FY2025 (+9% YoY) and the relatively long weighted average contract length of 19 months.

This is significantly aided by the growing Free Cash Flow generation of $920.29M (+3.7% YoY), the healthy balance at a net cash position of $1.09B (-6% YoY), and inherently zero debts whatsoever – allowing the SaaS company to weather the near-term macro uncertainties and potentially lumpy billings growth over the next few quarters.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

Based on DOCU's preliminary FY2025 overall revenue guidance of $3.13B at the midpoint (+5% YoY), the adj operating margins of 28.3% (-1.5 points YoY), the US statutory tax rate at 21%, and the shares outstanding of 212.5M, we are looking at an estimated FY2025 adj EPS of $3.29 (-7.3% YoY).

Despite the supposed bottom-line headwinds on a YoY basis, attributed to the impact from cloud migration, one-time litigation reserve in Q2'25, and ongoing shift in cash compensation versus equity, it is apparent that the SaaS company is likely to continue delivering a profitable growth investment thesis ahead.

These reasons may also be why the consensus has already raised their forward estimates, with DOCU expected to chart an accelerated top/bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +7.9%/+8.1% through FY2028.

This is compared to the original estimates of +5.6%/+1.6%, while building upon the historical 6Y growth at a CAGR of +27.3%/+84.5%, respectively.

DOCU Valuations

These reasons may also be why the market continues to award DOCU with stable FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 21.47x, compared to the 1Y mean of 20.31x, albeit noticeably moderated from its 5Y mean of 104.62x.

Thanks to the bottom-line growth acceleration, it is apparent that the SaaS company is rather attractive at an FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio of 0.93x as well, compared to the 5Y mean of 2.77x, the sector median of 1.40x, and its enterprise SaaS peers, including Adobe (ADBE) at 1.16x, Zoom (ZM) at 2.96x, and Microsoft (MSFT) at 2.46x.

This reason is also why we believe that DOCU remains compelling, thanks to the recent selloff, with it offering interested investors with an excellent margin of safety.

So, Is DOCU Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

DOCU 7Y Stock Price

For now, DOCU has lost -28.1% or the equivalent -$5.95B of its market cap from the 52-week heights, albeit seemingly well-supported at the prior support levels and 200-day moving averages of $74 ranges.

Based on our estimated FY2025 adj EPS of $3.29 as discussed above (-7.3% YoY) and the current FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 21.47x, it is apparent that the stock is still not too far from our fair value estimates of $70.60.

Based on the consensus FY2028 adj EPS of $4.49, there remains an excellent upside potential of +24.4% to our long-term price target of $96.40 as well, with it underscoring why the recent meltdown has been a boon.

Combined with the moderating short interest volume from 2024 heights, the lower CBOE Volatility Index, and the bullish support at the recent $70 bounce, we are reiterating our Buy rating for the DOCU stock here.

data.title: Alimentation Couche-Tard: A Growing Profit Powerhouse Delivering Strong Cash Flows
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Alimentation Couche-Tard (TSX:ATD:CA) is a wonderful business. Their gas stations and convenience stores are present across the globe, with the firm extracting significant profitability from what is often a cutthroat business environment.

Third-quarter results suggest same-store sales have plateaued due to the strain consumers are feeling on their pocketbooks. Nevertheless, ATD continues to pursue a business strategy focused on acquisitions, which should see the firm continue to acquire smaller competitors over the coming years.

My DCF analysis suggests the stock could be worth up to $90 per share, which would imply that an undervaluation of 23% is present.

I, therefore, rate the firm a Buy at the present time and have initiated a small position in the stock.

Economic Moat & Business Overview

ATD is one of the largest convenience store and gas station operators in North America and Europe, with the Québécois company operating a majority of its stores under the well-known Circle K and Couche-Tard banners.

The firm has expanded internationally over the past decade with multiple acquisitions of smaller rivals, seeing ATD now also become a dominant player in markets across the European continent.

The core of their business is the sale of what the company dubs “Road Transportation Fuel”, otherwise known as gas and diesel. Approximately 50% of all revenues are sourced from the sale of fuel to customers, with the other half coming from merchandise sales inside the petrol stations.

The sale of gasoline itself is an unforgiving business due to fuel being essentially a homogenous product, while the taxation, legislation, and competition in the industry only amplify this reality.

This impacts the operating margin for fuel sales, which at ATD is about 8%.

In order to produce larger margins and returns from operations, ATD has a vast network of increasingly larger stores that accompany their fuel pumps. Many of these convenience stores have much higher unit prices for products such as snacks, alcohol, or nonperishable foods than supermarkets or the likes of Walmart (WMT) because customers are willing to pay for the immediate availability (the elimination of needing to drive to another store) of these products.

These merchandise sales are much more profitable for ATD, with the segment operating margin being just shy of 20%.

Considering the simple nature of operating a gas station and shop, I believe the moat for these service stations arises from scale advantages, network effects, and any potential switching costs the operator is able to create for customers.

ATD certainly enjoys massive economies of scale, which help the firm negotiate better prices with suppliers of merchandise. I don’t think this advantage translates to fuel purchasing, as the ultimate supply price of both gasoline and diesel is dictated by the price of crude oil.

Nevertheless, the scale advantage ATD has compared to smaller rivals such as Murphy’s USA (MUSA) or Casey’s General Stores (CASY) in their convenience store operations is powerful as it allows for larger margins due to the increased operational efficiency they are able to achieve.

I also think ATD’s massive network of gas stations both in North America and across Europe generates a moat for the company, as the density of stations increases the likelihood that customers stop and refill or shop at their locations.

The massive international network of stations also compounds the benefits of customer retention activities employed by ATD, such as their Circle K rewards app. By providing repeat customers with discounts on gas or merchandise, ATD is able to create switching costs by encouraging loyalty.

With these factors in mind, I believe Alimentation Couche-Tard has a narrow economic moat built upon its convenience store cost efficiencies and the benefits its extensive network and customer loyalty programs drive.

I also think it is prudent to mention that ATD is currently exploring the acquisition of Seven & I Holdings, which would see the company acquire the entire 7-Eleven franchise of convenience stores.

The move has been met with resistance from the current management team at Seven & I (OTCPK:SVNDY) while also facing significant regulatory hurdles due to antitrust challenges in the U.S. Considering the unpredictable nature of the current U.S. presidential administration and the historic reluctance for Japanese companies to submit to foreign takeovers, I believe it is simply too early to say whether or not this deal may go through.

If it does, ATD would become the largest convenience store player in the world but also become saddled with the debt necessary to accomplish such a purchase. It would also most likely require ATD to divest some of their U.S. assets, which are highly profitable. In comparison, Seven & I have struggled to be profitable over the past decade, and thus the merger may substantially decrease margins at least temporarily at ATD.

Financial Analysis

The firm has focused massively on optimizing every activity performed at the company in the name of maximizing both the customer experience and profitability.

This strategy appears to be working well with the firm’s five-year average ROA, ROE, and ROIC resting at 9%, 21%, and 13%, respectively. Such wonderful returns illustrate just how efficiently ATD employs resources, equity, and capital.

The sizable ROIC in particular is well beyond their current WACC, which is only 6% thus supporting my thesis that the firm certainly enjoys the benefits of a tangible economic moat.

Gross, operating and net margins of 11%, 5.5%, and 4%, respectively are quite acceptable considering the relatively low margin nature of road transportation fuel sales and the massive levels of competition that manifest in the convenience store and gas station industries.

Alimentation Couche-Tard recently reported third-quarter results for 2025, which overall were quite strong for the Canadian firm.

Total revenues increased 6.5% on a YoY basis thanks to robust increases of 5% and 7.6% in their merchandise revenues and road transportation fuel sales, respectively. The massive 45% YoY increase in European sales came thanks to the acquisition of Total Energies and MAPCO service station assets.

Importantly, the increased proportion of European assets should allow ATD to boost its operating margin, as their European merchandise gross and operating margins are the largest of any region at 39% and about 5.5%, respectively.

Sales were basically flat on a same-store sales basis, which Alimentation’s management noted was due to persisting cost pressures on consumers and what is ultimately a relatively expensive price on crude oil.

I think now is also a good moment to share my findings about how gas stations tend to fare during falling oil prices and recessions. For some context, fuel station companies like ATD buy fuel at the spot price, which is dictated by global oil markets, and then sell it onwards to consumers at the pump price.

As a result, a period of falling oil prices (often accompanied by recessionary environments) will actually allow firms like ATD to sell with a greater margin as the spot price is lower than the current pump price due to the time lag present in this transformation.

With this in mind, I am not particularly concerned about the impact a recession may have on ATD’s revenues and profitability. While a short-term drop in earnings could arise should there be a particularly aggressive macroeconomic shock, falling gasoline and diesel prices can actually cause people to drive more and thus frequent service stations even more often.

Regardless, the significant expansion caused operating expenses to increase 8% YoY to CAD$2.1 billion. I think additional synergies will be achieved once the new stations are fully incorporated into the ATD supply chain, although even the current situation allowed operating income to increase by 4.4% to $968 million.

ATD’s company-wide operating margin for Q3 was 4.6%, which is essentially their long-term average.

Overall, I was very pleased with the third-quarter results as it shows just how well Alimentation Couche-Tard continues to manage their acquisitions while maintaining excellent operating economics.

I am also happy to report that on the balance sheet, ATD faces no major concerns despite their relatively large debt profile.

Multiple acquisitions over the last two decades have seen ATD take on a total of $20.4 billion in long-term debt. However, this debt is structured well at an average cost of 3.94% with an average maturity of 11 years. The bulk of the debt is essentially due post-FY30. However, it is worth noting that about $2.5 billion will come due by FY30 which the firm remains open to refinancing at attractive rates should they manifest themselves in the market.

While I generally prefer lower levels of debt, ATD has a proven track record of integrating acquisitions quickly and effectively into their operations, while making sure that financing of maturities is done astutely.

Alimentation is also an absolute cash-generation powerhouse with the firm producing a whopping $1.71 billion in FCF in FY24. Such massive cash flows should allow the firm to pay maturities with ease if refinancing isn’t attractive at the time.

Considering the operation as a whole, I must admit I am quite impressed with this convenience store and gas station operator. Their opportunistic approach to acquiring fragmented assets while focusing diligently on operational efficiencies, the customer experience, and how best to target each geographic market (by not eliminating local brand names and keeping some well-loved banners) increases my conviction that future expansion will be executed well.

Valuation

I like to start my valuation analysis using Seeking Alpha’s wonderful Quant-derived Valuation tab for Alimentation Couche-Tard’s stock, even if on this occasion there is no specific letter grade.

The TTM P/E GAAP of roughly 18x is quite high considering the firm is only growing at around 5% per annum. Then again, this is only slightly above the long-term average P/E of the S&P500 of 17x so it might be quite acceptable given their expansion objectives.

A P/S TTM of just 0.62x adds some much-needed context to the current situation, as it shows that total sales are being discounted almost 40% relative to the current valuation. Of course, the P/CF of 9.91x is certainly suggestive of a dearer valuation, but based on these relative valuation metrics, I believe there could be a reasonable value opportunity present in the shares.

Taking a peek at Seeking Alpha’s 5Y advanced chart for ATD:CA stock provides a nice illustration of the relative strength of shares, with the gas station giant almost outperforming the ever-popular S&P500 index (SPY).

Worth noting here is that ATD stock managed to match the U.S. markets, all the while only having one-third of the exposure to this geography. I think this ability to generate superb returns and growth with little exposure to what has become an increasingly turbulent and unpredictable U.S. market is impressive.

I also completed a DCF valuation in order to better gauge the present intrinsic value of shares relative to the current value of all future cash flows.

I sourced the FCF and balance sheet data from the financials tab for ATD stock and calculated that their average annualized unlevered FCF growth rate over the last decade was 16.5%. In order to err on the side of caution, I reduced this rate to just 6% for my projection period as I suspect the rate of mergers may slow down slightly if a recessionary environment were to arise.

I used a terminal growth rate of 3% to match what I suspect will be a hotter rate of inflation and GDP growth in North America and Europe, while my discount rate of 7.98% is the annualized 93-year average return produced by the popular S&P500. This benchmark rate is essentially my opportunity cost of not investing in the market, and one which I would therefore demand any alternative pick would outperform.

With these inputs, I calculated an IV of $90.68 per share, which implies a current undervaluation is present in the stock to the tune of about 23%.

While short-term (1–12 month) market events including the unpredictable nature of President Trump’s trade wars may have a real impact on ATD’s share price, I believe the long-term (1–10 years) outlook for ATD is very positive. The firm is excelling in execution while simultaneously growing the firm through meaningful acquisitions that should only further enhance its economic moat.

Risks facing Alimentation Couche-Tard

From my research, I have deduced that the primary risks facing the firm arise from its exposure to oil prices and from the regulatory environment surrounding the sale and burning of gasoline or diesel.

Given that the firm essentially accepts the market spot price for fuel, ATD’s earnings are susceptible to fluctuations as a result of fluctuating oil prices. Interestingly, I actually believe a significant spike in oil prices would be worse for the firm, as a rapidly rising spot price means pump prices actually lag what the firm is paying for new fuels.

Furthermore, if oil prices increase leading to a rise in the price of pump gas, shoppers may drive less, thus frequenting the lucrative convenience stores less too as they are brought to the pump less often.

Then again, a deep recession could leave consumers simply unable to afford to drive, which would once again reduce demand at ATD’s pumps and also cause merchandise sales to fall.

The next 10 years will also see Alimentation having to deal with what is becoming an increasingly electrified transportation environment. While it appears that the transition to BEVs has slowed down across the globe, I think the reality is that the internal combustion engine is slowly but surely going to go the way of the dodo. As a result, companies like ATD will have to offer even more EV charging infrastructure to customers in order to retain their business.

While ATD is already leading the charge (quite literally) into EV infrastructure, especially in Europe, I fear that the massive capital required to develop competitive charging locations may place a damper on earnings and returns.

With these factors in mind, I feel it is necessary to consider ATD to harbor a high degree of risk, which is why I urge all investors to conduct their own extensive research into these matters before making or forming any decisions about the company’s shares.

Summary

Alimentation Couche-Tard is a wonderful convenience store and gas station company. Their operational profile is impressive, with the firm extracting wonderful returns despite being in a hugely competitive and tough industry.

I was happy with the third-quarter earnings release and believe the current slowdown in same-store sales has more to do with the prevailing and unpredictable macroenvironment rather than an inherent weakness at the Canadian firm. Furthermore, the increased expansion into Europe combined with the reduced exposure to the U.S. relative to some of their competitors may help ATD continue to generate reasonable earnings even in the short term.

The valuation is also quite attractive, with what could be a 23% undervaluation further sweetening the deal for value-oriented investors.

Considering the firm and the valuation, I feel comfortable assigning a Buy rating at the present time and have personally taken a stake in the firm worth 10% of my portfolio.

data.title: Institutional, Retail Investors Shed $27B In US Stocks In Early April Sell-Off
data.description:
Institutional and retail investors offloaded a combined net of $27 billion in US stocks in early April — much of which was bought by hedge funds, index funds and exchange-traded funds — as financial markets whipsawed after the US introduced a slate of new tariffs but subsequently delayed their enforcement for 90 days.

Institutional investors sold $19.52 billion in stocks from April 2, when the tariffs were announced, through April 10, the day after the White House issued an executive order pausing the implementation of the tariffs, according to S&P Global Market Intelligence data. This selling outpaced the $18.88 billion in full-month net capital outflows from institutional investors in March. However, this was still below the average $35.92 billion in monthly equity offloading recorded over the last 12 months amid a prolonged selling trend.

Similarly, retail investors extended recent selling trends by adding another $7.56 billion in equity supply during the period, bringing the combined stock sell-off from retail and institutional investors to about $27 billion.

"Prior to the new administration taking office, institutions were shifting sector exposure to passive investment vehicles," said Thomas McNamara, a director for issuer solutions at Market Intelligence. "The group likely wanted to maintain market exposure while eradicating stock-specific risk as the new administration implemented its policies. So, we can infer that the group was trading cautiously, but they likely did not anticipate the volatility we have seen so far in April."

Hedge funds added a net $8.05 billion in equities while index funds and exchange-traded funds (ETFs) bought a net $9.07 billion during the seven trading days between April 2 and April 10, combining for about $17.12 billion in stock holdings to absorb most of the institutional investor sell-off. Index fund and ETF activity during the period extended on trends seen over the last 12 months. However, hedge fund buying in early April and in March contrasted with a monthly average of $466 million sold over the last 12 months.

Trends amid policy changes

US President Donald Trump announced the reciprocal tariff plan near market close on April 2. The original intent was to enforce a universal 10% tariff on all countries beginning April 5 and higher, country-specific rates on select countries beginning April 9. The move triggered a global stock market sell-off. The White House later issued an executive order on April 9 that maintained the 10% duty but delayed the higher rates for 90 days. The S&P 500 rebounded 9.52% in response that day.

Institutional investor activity was consistent during April 2–10, with net capital outflows recorded in six of the seven trading days during the period. The group sold a net $1.06 billion on April 2, a net $14.76 billion from April 3 to 8 following the tariff announcement, and a net $3.70 billion from April 9 to 10 in the days after the tariff delay was confirmed.

Retail investors were net stock sellers during four of the seven trading days, but cumulative activity was similar to that of institutional investors. Retail traders shed $916 million on April 2, $5.64 billion from April 3 to 8 and $1.01 billion from April 9 to 10.

Net capital flow changes by investor group in early April ($M)

Cumulative (April 2–10)

In contrast, index funds and ETFs were net buyers of equities during all three periods, adding $1.31 billion on April 2, $751.9 million from April 3 to 8 and $7 billion from April 9 to 10.

Hedge fund activity was mixed, with investors buying a net $1.43 billion on April 2 and $13.07 billion from April 3 to 8. Hedge funds reversed course on April 9 with significant selling, returning to a net buying position on April 10, but cumulative activity over these two days amounted to net capital outflows of $6.45 billion.

"[Hedge funds] were quick to capitalize on the downturn and the subsequent rally that ensued following the pause, and likely made a notable amount of money in doing so," McNamara said. "It would be short-term market dynamics that motivate hedge fund trading in this type of environment, and not necessarily any type of long-term play."

Trends by sector

The financials sector saw the biggest swings in investor positioning activity on April 2. Institutional investors dumped $1.17 billion in financial company stocks, but hedge funds gobbled up $1.27 billion in these holdings.

From April 3 to 8, institutional investors largely rotated out of cyclical sectors such as industrials, financials, IT and consumer discretionary, with reciprocal buying volumes by hedge funds.

How investor groups changed their sector holdings in early April ($M)

Cumulative (April 2–10)

Index fund and ETF investors were the most significant buyers from April 9 to 10. The data shows net capital inflows for this group in nine out of 11 sectors, with the largest buying volumes seen among IT and healthcare stocks.

For the full period from April 2 to 10, institutional investors were net sellers in eight sectors but pursued modest buying activity in noncyclical sectors. Retail investors were net sellers in every sector except IT.

"Institutional investors [exhibited a risk-off stance] by shifting capital from active long-only accounts to ETFs, favoring diversified exposure over individual stock risk," McNamara said. "Institutions primarily sold in higher-risk sectors ... while reallocating to safer sectors like real estate, materials and utilities. This behavior underscores a collective move toward capital preservation amid market uncertainties, such as tariffs and rising bond yields."

Hedge fund investors saw net capital inflows in eight sectors, with most allocations toward the cyclical financials and consumer discretionary sectors. Index fund and ETF investors similarly increased their net holdings in eight sectors, though net allocations differed slightly from those of hedge funds. However, the two groups were both net buyers within the communication services, consumer staples, financials, industrials, IT and materials sectors.

Original Post

data.title: Consensus Price Forecasts - Tariff Shifts Jolt Gold Price In Record-Setting Run
data.description:
The Take

Mercurial developments in the global trade landscape, as the US' expanding tariff plans spark reciprocal measures, continued to take centerstage across metals markets in March. Gold reached multiple record highs as economic uncertainties increased interest in safe-haven assets. US recession fears, a weaker US dollar, pockets of supply tightness and inventory building amid worries of impending trade barriers bolstered industrial metals prices, but concerns for the impact of constrained trade flows on demand levels and a lingering supply overhang for some metals kept downside risks intact. Consensus gold price targets for the 2025–29 period have been upgraded, while expectations for industrial metals — except for cobalt and zinc — were adjusted lower.

Tariff announcements from the US and vows of retaliatory actions from other countries continued to agitate metals markets in March, supercharging safe-haven demand for gold as the uncertainty induced by escalating trade tensions threatens to curb global economic growth. Although major central banks have continued to trim policy rates, economic growth prospects are capped by the potential adverse implications of heightening trade restrictions, which have subsequently come to pass, following President Donald Trump's reciprocal tariff announcements on April 2. The US expanding tariffs on most of its trading partners have the potential to jeopardize even its own economic growth, feeding into recession fears and dragging down the US dollar. The trade-weighted index of the US dollar dropped to 102.07 on April 3, down 6.7% year to date. Reflecting market apprehension, the US manufacturing purchasing managers' index (PMI) returned to contractionary territory in March, following two consecutive months of expansion. In its Federal Open Market Committee meeting in March, the US Federal Reserve kept interest rates unchanged, even as it trimmed its US economic growth outlook for 2025 to 1.7% from 2.1% and raised its core inflation projection to 2.8% from 2.5%.

In China, manufacturing PMIs expanded at faster rates in March, suggesting resilience in the face of hiked US import duties. Other indicators echo this uptick in activity, as the January–February industrial production in China rose 5.9% year over year while fixed asset investment climbed 4.1% and retail sales increased 4.0%. Although China announced new incentives in mid-March to further spur economic activity, a lack of detail around the stimulus and, at the time, pending US tariff, capped market optimism.

After initially breaching $3,000 per ounce on March 18, the London Bullion Market Association gold price consecutively broke records toward the close of the month to settle at $3,115.10/oz on March 31. The unpredictability of Trump's tariff rollouts and possible retaliation from other countries have fueled concerns over inflationary pressures and fears of a possible US recession, driving investors' flight to safety. Unease over the US economy permeated the market, lifting COMEX gold inventories to a historic high of 43.8 million ounces as of March 31.

Trade tensions compound still-evolving geopolitical risks stemming from the Russia-Ukraine war and Middle East conflicts. Meanwhile, heightened activity in the physical market has also been a critical force driving the gold rally. Central bank buying picked up pace in January, and the total assets under management of physically backed gold exchange-traded funds (ETFs) reached an all-time high at above $300 billion. Given the sharp price increases of late, consensus price targets for gold are somewhat lagging current market values, despite being raised by an average of 1.1% for 2025–27; they are 0.5% lower for 2028–29 when profit-taking is expected to limit the upside.

Surging gold prices, safe-haven demand and market tightness combined to drive support for the silver price, which reached its highest level since October 2024 at $33.80/oz on March 13. As trade tensions escalate, silver has remained an attractive option as a hedge against uncertainty. Tight fundamentals add to the bullishness, as supply continues to lag demand that is largely driven by industrial applications, including uses in renewable energy and electrification. However, sensitivity to economic conditions could drive headwinds, as a slowdown in industrial activity could dampen silver demand going forward. The silver consensus price outlook has been upgraded 0.6% on average over the five-year forecast horizon.

Platinum and palladium prices were range-bound between $900/oz and $1,000/oz throughout March. Consensus price expectations for 2025–29 have been downgraded by 0.7% and 0.6% on average, respectively. Given the heavy exposure of platinum group metals (PGM) demand to the automotive sector, tariff concerns have likewise swayed PGMs, though the impact is likely to be more pronounced for platinum than palladium. The World Platinum Investment Council considers the US to be "almost self-sufficient" in palladium but short in platinum. Worries over the flurry of US tariff announcements have given rise to significant inflows of platinum into NYMEX warehouses, which could accelerate the tightening of supplies. Outlooks point to a continued supply deficit in 2025 for platinum but an emerging surplus for palladium in the same year. New US tariffs on imports of all automobiles that went into effect April 3 pose downside risk to demand, with the automotive sector being the single largest demand segment for both platinum and palladium, given their application in catalytic converters for exhausts of internal combustion engine vehicles. The uptake of electric vehicles that have no need for autocatalysts could also erode demand levels for PGMs over the medium to long term.

The London Metal Exchange three-month (LME 3M) copper price rose to a five-month high of $9,955 per metric ton on March 19 and remained supported above $9,800/t through month-end amid US dollar weakness and worries that the red metal could be the next target of US tariffs. An executive order issued in late February called for an investigation into US copper imports under Section 232 of the Trade Expansion Act — a likely forerunner to the imposition of US trade barriers — which grants the US president the authority to "adjust imports" of goods for national security reasons, such as supply chain vulnerabilities. As this unfolded, COMEX copper prices surged to an all-time high of $11,210/t on March 20, while the arbitrage between COMEX and LME cash prices reached $1,299/t. The COMEX premium could encourage metal flow into the US and prompt concerns over supply disruptions elsewhere. Copper concentrate spot treatment charges (TCs) reached a record low of negative $21.10/t in March, suggesting a persistent concentrate squeeze that could result in refined output cuts. Smelter capacity expansions in China, Indonesia, India and the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC), alongside a new round of stimulus measures in China and signs of recovery in the domestic economy, exacerbated supply concerns. Nevertheless, the adverse implications of tariffs on demand and global trade remain a downside risk. Copper consensus price outlooks have been lowered 0.2% on average for 2025–29.

The weakening US dollar and news of refined production curtailments bolstered the LME 3M zinc price, which reached a monthly high of $2,972/t on March 25. Concerns over the latest US tariffs have prompted US consumers to build their zinc inventories. Although US imports of refined zinc fell 9.1% month over month in January, rising demand has propped up the US zinc premium to the LME in March. The Platts-assessed US Midwest delivered Special High Grade zinc premium rose to 21 cents per pound at the end of March. Platts is part of S&P Global Commodity Insights. While contractionary US manufacturing PMI for March reflects businesses' apprehension about the US economy, expansionary China PMIs suggested beneficial impacts of recent incentives, and additional stimulus packages aim to boost economic activity further. The concentrate market remains tight in terms of supply, as signaled by the annual contract agreement for TCs settled between Teck Resources Ltd. and China's Nandan Nanfang Non-Ferrous Metals Smelt Co. Ltd. at $60/t, which is 64% lower than the 2024 benchmark of $165/t. Citing the squeeze in raw materials, major zinc producer Nyrstar NV announced it will trim production by 25% at its Hobart smelter in Australia in April. The impact of reduced refined output, however, could be short-lived, considering a robust mine supply pipeline. Consensus price forecasts for zinc are 0.2% higher on average for 2025–29.

The LME 3M closing nickel price reached a near five-month high March 12 at $16,641/t but pared back gains to end the month at $15,918/t. US recession fears have weighed on the dollar and triggered a round of short-covering. On March 14, investment funds turned net long on LME nickel for the first time since October 2024. Profit-taking curbed the upside, while supply-side fundamentals continued to weigh on market sentiment. LME nickel inventories exceeded 200,000 metric tons on March 13 for the first time in over 3.5 years, helped largely by inflows of China-origin metal. China refined class 1 nickel production climbed 17.3% year over year in the January–February period. Indonesian production is expected to expand further in 2025, albeit at a slower pace due to tight ore availability and potential regulatory changes. An ongoing supply overhang is keeping the consensus price outlook biased lower, with price expectations downgraded 1.0% on average for 2025–29.

After trading range-bound between $2,600/t and $2,700/t for most of March, the LME 3M aluminum price fell past the lower boundary to end the month at $2,533/t. Cash prices traded at a premium to 3M prices, as a tight spot market helped to prop up market sentiment. Despite sanctions on Russian imports in key economies, the low India-origin metal at LME warehouses has resulted in increasing withdrawals of Russia-origin metal. The Platts-assessed benchmark US Midwest premium rose to a record-high of 41.75 cents/lb in late February, with the approaching implementation of US tariffs on steel and aluminum imports. While the premium has since corrected, renewed upside remains a possibility as tariff developments continue to unfold. Despite signs of tightening supply, broadening barriers to trade continue to dampen sentiment. Aluminum consensus price expectations are down 0.2% on average for the 2025–29 period.

Short-term supply disruptions drove the Platts-assessed European cobalt metal price to its highest level since August 2023 at $17.5/lb on March 13, but it has since pulled back to $16/lb on March 31. The export ban enforced by DRC in late February has triggered a shortage in the cobalt physical market, as participants opted to preserve inventories. Dwindling cobalt stocks at the LME prompted the exchange to temporarily suspend lending requirements on cobalt contracts on March 6 to encourage more inflows into its warehouses. The export ban — designed to address global oversupply — has an initial implementation period of four months and is subject to review for possible extension.

The imminent release of stockpiled inventories once the ban is lifted allay supply worries, however. Although there was an uptick in EV sales in February, battery manufacturers have also begun to shift away from cobalt-containing batteries in favor of the less costly lithium-iron-phosphate ones. Despite challenges in the US, the transition to a green economy remains a crucial demand driver for cobalt alongside other battery metals. Cobalt consensus price targets have been upgraded 4.5% on average for 2025–29.

After testing the $110 per dry metric ton threshold in late February, the Platts IODEX 62% Fe iron ore price slumped in March to average $102.43/dmt amid easing weather-related supply disruptions and a souring China demand outlook. Iron ore shipments from Australia have resumed in full, and major miners have maintained their annual production guidance for 2025 despite operational outages due to cyclones earlier in the year. Meanwhile, China iron ore imports declined 8.4% in January–February under pressure from a fall in steel production, with further cuts expected on weak domestic steel demand and external trade pressure. Antidumping measures imposed by Vietnam and South Korea on China's steel exports have been stifling demand prospects, in addition to newly imposed US duties on all steel and aluminum imports and tariff hikes on China-origin goods. Tepid demand will exacerbate the overcapacity in China's steel industry — China's National Development and Reform Commission has expressed an intention to reduce steel output to address this issue. While this intervention would improve mill margins, it would also soften iron ore demand and aggravate the market surplus. The iron ore consensus price outlook is downgraded 0.7% on average for the five-year forecast horizon.

Original Post
