data.title: Hess Corporation: A Lot Of Solid Attributes And Growth Opportunities To Support Its Rebound
data.description:
Despite a volatile market environment, many oil producers remain strategically positioned to stabilize their demand and revenue growth amid the falling oil prices. As Guyana emerges as one of the key sources of high-quality energy commodities, Hess Corporation (NYSE: HES) has become an even more compelling stock today. It also maintains an excellent financial positioning despite its capital intensity and continued expansion. This makes a highly sustainable and secure company despite the looming market risks. In addition, the stock appears cheap today compared to previous years and suggests an upside. This may be an excellent time for adding this to your portfolio.

HES Q4 2024: A Sweet Ending

The expansion of Guyana's economy propelled by energy commodities has been evident in recent years. With its vast oil reserves, many explorers and producers enjoy growth, profitability, and sustainability. The same can be seen in Hess Corporation considering its strong topline growth at the end of the previous year.

Its operating revenue amounted to $3.2B, up by 6.4% YoY from $3.0B. It also exceeded its Q3 revenue by 0.9%. For the whole year, it reached $ B, a % YoY increase. This shows HES' sustained growth, albeit its toppish trend during the second half. And I think this is still impressive considering the weaker oil prices with or without hedging.

Various aspects contributed to its resilient growth in Q4. One of these is its high production per day of oil and natural gas. It enjoyed most of these in Guyana, with crude oil volume per day increasing by 52% YoY and 15% QoQ. Another aspect is its strategic pricing, which allowed it to stay competitive and sustain its demand. So, its increased sales volume compensated for the lower price.

Third, Guyana's and Malaysian oil markets remained resilient, as shown by their relatively higher and stable crude oil prices. Considering the continued decrease in Brent and WTI prices, HES had some level of flexibility. Fourth, its production is well-diversified. It has various locations extending from the Americas to Southeast Asia. Lastly, its natural gas liquids had increased demand and prices and supported revenue growth.

What's even more interesting about HES is its improved efficiency and lower cost sensitivity. Its operating leverage dropped from 39.9% to 29.8%, which is impressive considering the inflation uptick in Q4. This also shows a lower variable cost contribution, showing it's well-positioned against inflation. Hence, it's not a surprise that its operating margin rose from 24.8% to 29.3% YoY.

Laggards

In most of the oil/gas-related stocks I covered, I specified the risks associated with tariffs, decreasing Brent and WTI prices, and geopolitical tensions. At this point, I would like to highlight other risks that we may have overseen as we remain confident with its operations.

Security Tension

Guyana and Venezuela dispute over Essequibo region, a resource-rich area. While it is legally under Guyana's jurisdiction, Venezuela continues to assert its 'right' in the region. We do note that this tension dates back to the 19th century, although it became dormant. However, the tension arose again in 2015 amid the discovery of vast oil reserves off the Guyana Coast. Note that a large portion of it is in its exclusive economic zone adjacent to the disputed region.

In December 2023, Venezuela had a controversial referendum, wherein it received solid support for the annexation of the Essequibo region.

Guyana must watch out for the potential escalation of this tension, which can still lead to a war. This can negatively impact the HES' operations from production and supply chain to its price and demand.

On a lighter note, I think this can still be avoided as the US and the UK, Guyana's primary investors intend to intervene if the tension persists. This can assure Guyana's security considering the political power and influence of these two giants.

Risk of Dutch Disease

The discovery of vast oil reserves in Guyana has led to a drastic expansion of its economy over the past decade. However, overreliance on oil can cause some risks. For instance, there may be more focus on oil, unlike other sectors like agriculture.

That is why the government must implement strict guidelines to prevent overutilization and exploitation of oil. This can prevent depletion which can send the economy crashing as oil is now its core growth driver. Thankfully, the Guyanese government maintains a strong regulatory framework that honors existing contracts with oil companies like Hess (HES) and ExxonMobil (XOM).

The government must also be prudent with its economic policies. That way, it can control its currency value. This can help manage the affordability of oil in other countries. In addition, it’s already working on diversifying its economy to cushion the impact of oil shocks.

Drivers

Despite the risks, I believe these aspects can protect it or drive its expansion.

Adequate Reserves

With its continued oil and gas exploration, HES maintains adequate reserves to sustain its operations. As of Q4 2024, it has proved reserves of 1.44B barrels of oil equivalent or boe. This is an increase of 247M boe from 1.37B boe in 2023, driven mainly by Guyana and the Bakken.

Considering its increasing proven reserves, the company is well-positioned against oil shocks. It has enough liquid reserves, giving it some pricing flexibility amid the weaker Brent price. This also ensures the sustainability of its business despite its continued expansion.

Well-Diversified Business

I like its well-diversified business model. I do not only refer to its oil and gas products. I also consider its geographical location. Instead of focusing on the US and Guyana, it also increases its presence in the Asia Pacific with its production in the North Malay Basin in Malaysia. Note that it’s the 28th country with the highest oil consumption. Thailand and Singapore, which are also its primary consumers rank 16 and 17.

This geographical diversification positions the company well against operational, currency fluctuation, and geopolitical risks. In addition, it provides more flexibility in its cost structure. For instance, Guyana’s low-cost and high-margin production of light-sweet crude offers more demand and profitability. In Malaysia, the fact that its economy continues to grow, HES may expect steady cash flow. In the US, HES has already established its operations with more mature operations and improved infrastructure. This makes it easy for the company to ramp up or lower its exploration and production activities. So, it’s not surprising to see its increasing proved reserves.

Moreover, its multiple locations make it easy for HES to export oil to different parts of the world. It can also stabilize oil and gas since it does not rely solely on the US. This limits the impact of DXY changes to keep the demand steady.

As we can see, its increasing revenue and margins show that its strategies continue to pay off.

Strong Balance Sheet and Cash Flows

Although it is capital-intensive, HES shows high liquidity and sustainability. Cash may be decreasing due to high E&P capex. But its relatively stable receivables and increasing inventory shows its operations have enough resources. We must also consider its increasing cash flow from operations, which is about 40% higher at $5.6B than in the previous year. Also, FCF improved from -$166M in 2023 to $654M in 2024. This shows that its increased E&P activities generated more cash returns.

In addition, most of its borrowings have longer maturities. More specifically, 56% of its borrowings have a maturity of five years and above. Only 10% of the total will mature within two years. What’s even more interesting is that its Net Debt/EBITDA remains very low at 1.02x. This means that the company only needs a year to cover all its borrowings. Considering its borrowing maturities and current cash and earnings, HES has a lot of time and resources to prepare for this.

Natural Gas

Its natural gas can support its growth and expansion for several reasons. One, the price is more stable than crude oil. Two, natural gas may sustain its demand as electricity consumption heavily relies on it. Three, the boom of data centers will require more energy sources, especially electricity, which may bolster the demand for natural gas in the long run. That is why I agree with the optimistic outlook of the US EIA in natural gas prices.

Chevron-Hess M&A: Positives and Negatives

This is an important topic to cover considering how massive the impact will be on HES’ shareholders.

An easy answer is that shareholders will see a higher value of their shares as Chevron (CVX) offers a premium acquisition price and has a solid dividend history. Whether they will stay or sell their shares after the M&A, there will be earnings depending on how much they bought HES before the acquisition. Another positive impact is that HES shareholders will have more assurance of financial stability and a more diversified exposure. CVX is a highly established company and one of the major players in the industry. Lastly, CVX is strategically positioned due to its established reputation, massive size, and stronger balance sheet. If you want to know more about CVX, you can check my recent analysis.

Meanwhile, one possible negative I note is tax implications since this M&A deal is a stock-for-stock exchange. HES shareholders may be subject to a higher capital gains tax. Another potential risk is that CVX appears to be more volatile as it fluctuates with global oil prices. This is in contrast to HES since it is deeply tied to Brent only, so fluctuations appear more manageable. Lastly, HES shareholders will become exposed to downstream risks. Some of these are lower margins compared to upstream. Note that most refineries are more sensitive to fuel demand swings and crude oil input costs. Also, downstream operations may depend on external factors, such as industrial activities, travel demand, and EV adoption. These things can affect oil, fuel, and gasoline demand and prices, so they will also affect revenues and expenses. Refining overcapacity must also be taken into account.

Price Ratio

HES has dramatically increased in the past five years. And I think that’s logical considering its primary metrics like the EPS and BVPS.

Currently, the stock trades at 14x its EPS. As you can see, I used two methods to weigh it carefully due to the negative value in 2020. One is by ignoring the 2020 EPS, so the four-year average shows that the stock trades at 26x the EPS. If we use this to get the fair value of the stock, the TP will be $237.74, an 86% upside potential. The other method is by smoothing the EPS in 2020, which will lead to a five-year average P/E of 17x. This gives a TP of $154.91, a 20% upside potential.

Concerning P/B, the stock trades at 3.48x its P/B, which is quite lower than the five-year average. This gives a TP of $143.49, a 13% upside potential.

To determine which is the better predictor, I also did a regression analysis for 20 observations. It turns out that P/B is a more reliable price predictor. It has a very high correlation with the price. This means that every time the BVPS changes, there is a 92% chance that the price will move in the same trend. Its p-value is also low at only 0.003, which tells us that only 0.3% of their relationship was random or just happened by chance. This proves the significance of using P/B to value the stock.

While these are historical values, the statistical results can help predict the direction of the price. It is easier to do every release of quarterly or annual reports.

DCF Model

I also used the DCF Model to assess the intrinsic value of the stock in a more forward-looking manner. Like the price ratios, the model suggests underpricing with a TP of $143.80, a 14% upside potential.

Like most of the stocks I covered, I set its perpetual growth rate at 2% for a conservative assumption. Meanwhile, I derived its WACC using the Capital Asset Pricing Model and set its risk premium at 5% to cover the risk to take in this stock.

Lastly, I derived the FCFF by deriving the NOPAT, capex, depreciation, and net working capital. Concerning NOPAT, I began with the revenue projection after setting its CAGR of 12%. Then I set the COGS (20%) and operating expenses (44%) as a percentage of revenue. For the net working capital, I accounted for all operating assets and liabilities. These are current assets and liabilities, excluding cash and borrowings. I computed accounts receivables, inventory, and accounts payable using DSO, DIO, and DPO. I estimated the remaining assets and liabilities by setting them as a percentage of revenue.

Technicals

HES has taken a nosedive without a warning. In only two weeks, it hit its 52-week low. However, RSI gave early warnings of a pullback and/or reversal as early as February. The price and RSI did not beat the previous high. As we can see, HES tried three times to create new highs this Q1 2025. Yet, the trading momentum of buyers weakened. The confirmation came out during the last week of March. Even if the price created a new high, RSI did not beat its previous high in January. This conveyed a bearish divergence. True enough, it set its resistance last April 1 but RSI was still weaker. After two days, a bearish fair value gap appeared and led to a sharp downtrend.

Now, the question is whether the market has overreacted. I think not. After all, technicals are not tied solely to market sentiments. Aside from market indicators, we must look at other strong predictors like Brent. The same day Brent had a steep drop, and HES followed. And I think that’s logical considering that Brent greatly affects not only the price and demand of HES’ crude oil but also the economy of Guyana since it’s propelled by the oil market. It can be a bit confusing since a lot of times HES and Brent went in opposite directions. But in my observation, a sharp drop or increase in Brent’s price affects market sentiments. Look at how HES rebounded from December to January when Brent went up again. To make it simple, every time there is a sharp turn of events affecting Brent, HES responds immediately. And I think that’s a logical reaction considering the volatile market environment.

Despite this, we are seeing new buying opportunities in this stock. It is very cheap today as it trades below the 50-day and 200-day SMA. But the 50-day SMA is still moving above the 200-day SMA. This confirms the recent market reaction and that a rebound is likely to happen soon. In addition, the stock is in the oversold region. The MACD and signal lines remain in the negative region. However, the histogram is starting to become less negative, showing a weakening selling momentum. We can see it in the closing price yesterday as it reflects market indecision.

Conclusion

Hess Corporation offers a lot of potential for investors with its well-diversified products and geographical location, sustainable proved reserves, and excellent Balance Sheet. Its acquisition by Chevron has some risks and rewards, but I think it will give more value to the shareholders. It will also shield the company from the weak points of Guyana’s economy. In addition, it is already underpriced after the unfavorable technical trends. Considering its upside potential and new buying opportunities, I am giving it a strong buy rating.

data.title: McDonald's: Operating Leverage Can Be Significant
data.description:
It’s been six months since I last wrote about McDonald's Corporation (NYSE:MCD). During this time, the company has released its annual results. There are many interesting things to discuss about the fiscal year 2024—and especially about the company’s future.

Since my last article, the company has appreciated by 3.37%, compared to a 10.18% decline in the index.

Thesis Recap

First, I’ll go over a few bullet points from the McDonald’s investment thesis discussed in my previous article.

"- Owning 80% of the real estate for its locations and 57% of the land allows McDonald's to use these assets as collateral for debt, enabling them to continue acquiring premium locations in cities. Thanks to their franchise model, this expansion happens more quickly, and franchisees pay them rent and royalties.

- The company plans to have 50,000 restaurants by 2027. As we know, franchised restaurants have significantly higher margins than those operated directly by the company (84% operating margin versus 16% for company-operated locations). Therefore, depending on how many of these types of restaurants they open, the company's overall operating margins will either increase or decrease."

Other important points to pay attention to are McDonald’s value proposition in its menus, the strength ratio in relation to its franchisees, and the expansion strategy aiming for 50,000 locations by 2027.

Just for you to remember:

"The Robustness Ratio, as I understand it based on the definition provided by Nick Sleep, is the untapped pricing power over your customer. In the case of McDonald's, the franchisees. We will understand it throughout the article." My first McDonald's article.

Looking at what’s happened this year, it’s the first time the “Royalty / Revenue per location” ratio has exceeded 13%. This is partly due to lower sales at franchised restaurants, but also because McDonald’s is starting to charge higher royalties to new franchisees.

As a result, we could see this ratio continue to rise—which would be a positive development, as long as the increases aren’t too abrupt and don’t damage McDonald’s reputation as a franchisor.

McDonald's Value Proposition

As I said in my previous article:

"McDonald's has always been characterized as one of the best fast-food alternatives in terms of value for money. The direction the company was taking in recent years didn’t make much sense to me (this was confirmed in one of their recent conferences), as they were focusing on introducing more "premium" burgers at higher price points."

From now on, it’s important to keep a close eye on how this aspect of the business evolves. McDonald’s tends to struggle when the low-income consumer is under pressure, which is why it needs to position itself as the go-to option for this segment in the fast food space.

The company has clearly recognized this over the past few quarters, and I like seeing that it continues to work hard to address it.

For example, the $5 Meal Deal has been a major success—it’s generating average tickets above $10, thanks to effective cross-selling opportunities. It’s working exactly as intended: boosting traffic and improving the perceived value.

Another popular initiative is the “Buy One, Add One for $1” offer, particularly strong in the breakfast segment. It’s giving customers a lot of flexibility to create personalized combos.

According to surveys, all of this is translating into improvements across the board: perceived value, affordability, and overall satisfaction with the most recent visit.

Expansion Plan

Since 2023, McDonald’s has been aiming to operate 50,000 restaurants by 2027. This year, the company added 1,655 locations, bringing the total to 43,477. For 2025, they expect to open between 1,800 and 2,200 new restaurants.

To reach 50,000 locations, the company would need to grow its store count at a CAGR of 4.8% over the next three years. Since I believe McDonald’s will continue closing company-operated restaurants in favor of franchised ones—which generate significantly less revenue but much higher margins—I expect the group’s total revenue to grow at around 5.3% over the next three to even five years.

To estimate McDonald’s future margins, we need to look at what percentage of the upcoming 50,000 locations will be franchised versus company-operated. It’s important to remember that franchised restaurants have operating margins of around 84%, compared to just 15–16% for company-operated locations.

Based on the current growth trends, I believe the percentages will look like the chart below, which—according to my calculations—would give McDonald’s operating margins of approximately 51%. We will use these figures in the valuation section.

Capital Allocation

I think McDonald’s capital allocation is very solid. The company returns more than 100% of its generated free cash flow to shareholders—the excess coming from debt issuance. Over the past seven years, McDonald’s has bought back about 8% of its outstanding shares.

It also pays a dividend that currently yields 2.3%, which is quite respectable for a company that’s expected to grow it by around 8–10% annually. The payout ratio is healthy, sitting around 60%.

As for M&A, which has accounted for about 7% of FCF, this is largely due to McDonald’s practice of repurchasing restaurants from franchisees—either to operate them directly or to resell them to more qualified franchise partners.

All of this falls under capital allocation if we're talking about FCF since McDonald's reinvests a lot of money into the business by buying land and opening company-owned restaurants, which are later leased to franchisees. It's like a disguised REIT. In fact, the reinvestment rate – including Capex, M&A, and Net Changes in Working Capital – averages 31% of NOPAT, but this figure is expected to be higher during these years of growth in new openings. In 2024, it was actually 42%.

Financials

​Taking advantage of McDonald's recent release of its annual results, let's review its financial figures for fiscal year 2024.

As we can see in the image, the company’s revenue has grown at a pace of 3.4% in recent years, but there’s an explanation for this. While earnings per share have grown by 7.1%, this has been driven by significant operating leverage (in 2018, the EBIT margin was 41%, and this year it reached 46%) and share buybacks (an 8% reduction in shares outstanding over the past seven years).

The reason McDonald’s has experienced flat revenue but significantly increasing margins is that, since 2021, it has been closing company-operated stores and opening more franchised locations. Franchise restaurants have EBIT margins of around 84%, compared to just 15–16% for company-operated ones. Even though revenue growth is more modest, McDonald’s profits are of much higher quality.

In fact, the difference has been particularly pronounced this year. The number of franchised locations increased by 4.4%, reaching 41,432, while company-operated restaurants decreased by 4.5%, down to 2,045 locations. Moreover, this number is expected to continue declining. It’s not that McDonald’s is closing these restaurants, but rather converting them into franchises—which generate lower revenue for the company, but significantly higher margins.

One aspect that concerns some analysts is the company’s debt. However, if we understand why it’s relatively high (2.68x net debt/EBITDA)—even though that’s below the average of recent years—we’ll gain a clearer picture of the business.

"MCD uses debt to purchase the locations and land on which it operates. With a large scale and predictability in its business, it obtains debt at very low rates (3.7%), and 96% of it is at fixed rates, so it is not affected by interest rate hikes in debt payments." My first article

If we look at McDonald’s returns, we see they’ve remained fairly stable over time. What’s encouraging is that the strategy of closing company-operated stores and expanding the franchised model to improve the company’s profitability profile appears to be working.

It’s also worth noting that McDonald’s is currently in an expansionary CAPEX cycle expected to continue through the end of 2027, with a target of reaching 50,000 restaurants. This has a temporary negative impact on returns, since the increase in CAPEX inflates the denominator, while the positive effects on the numerator haven’t fully materialized yet.

As a result, McDonald’s future returns will likely be higher than they are today—possibly even approaching 30%.

In fact, when it comes to CAPEX, the company stated it plans to increase capital expenditures by $300–500 million per year—pushing the CAPEX-to-sales ratio up to around 13%. Beyond that point, visibility becomes more limited, but historically, during periods of controlled expansion, CAPEX has hovered around 8–9% of sales.

So, starting in 2027, we should expect to see an increase in cash generation and improved returns, just as I mentioned above.

This increase in CAPEX is having a clear impact on free cash flow. The company has stated a target of achieving a 90% FCF-to-net income ratio, but this is now the third consecutive year they’ve fallen short—and in fact, the ratio has been declining. They don’t expect to reach the target next year either, although they do anticipate some improvement.

That said, I believe that after 2027, they’ll be able to comfortably meet–or even exceed–that 90% target. Alternatively, we could use a normalized FCF with maintenance Capex (using D&A) instead of expansion Capex. With this approach, the FCF margin would improve by a couple of percentage points (to 28% of revenues), reaching $7.2B.

Valuation

McDonald’s is currently trading at 21x EBIT. Its historical multiple has been around 18x, but it’s important to consider that McDonald’s margins and returns are expected to improve following its expansion plan, so I believe it deserves a higher exit multiple.

With everything mentioned in the article, I arrive at this model. I believe McDonald’s sales – due to the franchising percentage I discussed earlier – can grow at a rate of around 5.3%, and that same factor could help improve EBIT margins to around 50–51%.

I also think that once the CAPEX expansion cycle ends, OCF conversion will improve from 80% to 90%, and CAPEX/sales will decline from its peak of 13.1% in 2027 to about 8.5% by 2029.

Taking all of this into account, I estimate FCF growth rates between 11% and 13%, which is quite solid considering the company’s stability. If we then add a 3.5% shareholder return – through buybacks and dividends – we’re looking at total returns of around 14% to 16% for McDonald’s, as long as it doesn’t experience a de-rating.

If that were to happen, our returns would be reduced—but since it's currently trading at 21x EBIT, and as I’ve explained, its fair value is now closer to 20x EBIT rather than the historical 18x, a potential de-rating wouldn’t have a major impact on our returns.

McDonald’s tends to be a stock that performs better during market downturns, and it's precisely in those moments that it generates alpha relative to the index. Considering the turbulent times ahead, it could serve as a strong portfolio anchor—that is why I rate the stock as a buy.

Important Graph

This is a chart I really like to include, and it holds even more weight in the case of McDonald's. Despite being considered a stable and mature business, McDonald's has managed to outperform the index over a 10-year period—and all of this with virtually no volatility.

If we look at the middle chart, we can see that over the last 10 years, McDonald’s has only dropped more than 16% once, and that was during March 2020. Not even the E. coli outbreak in the third quarter – which I discussed in my third article – was enough to push it below those levels.

All of this has led to McDonald's average P/E ratio sitting around 25x, despite not having explosive growth. But as we've pointed out throughout the article, even though top-line growth hasn’t been massive due to the closure of company-owned restaurants, operating income has grown at a faster pace, and its EBIT margin keeps expanding.

Risks

Aside from systemic risks for the sector – such as the E. coli incident they experienced in the third quarter – I believe the biggest current risk for McDonald’s is the weakening of the American consumer.

I’ve already mentioned how important it is for McDonald’s to be a strong value-for-money option—focusing on "value products" and avoiding competition in higher-priced categories, as I don't believe it has the brand image to support that positioning.

The whole issue around tariffs could trigger a second wave of inflation, which would significantly hurt lower-income American consumers. According to the University of Michigan’s consumer sentiment indicator, confidence among this group is at its lowest point since 2022 due to ongoing uncertainty. Discretionary spending – like dining out – could be one of the first things they cut from their budgets.

The positive side is that McDonald’s is becoming less and less dependent on actually selling burgers, but part of the royalties it charges its franchisees is still based on their sales. This incentivizes franchisees to keep growing, but it could also end up having a negative impact – although I believe only a mild one – on revenue from that stream.

Conclusion

I believe McDonald’s is a great company that continues to improve over time. The new expansion plan should have a positive impact on its margins and returns, and its FCF is expected to grow at a mid-low double-digit rate. The key will be to closely monitor the macroeconomic situation in the United States, especially how it affects the American consumer.

data.title: 3 Ways To Profit From China's Export Ban On Rare Earth Metals
data.description:
Rare earth minerals, or rare earth elements (REEs), are a group of 17 chemically similar elements: the 15 lanthanides (lanthanum through lutetium), plus scandium and yttrium. Despite their name, they’re not always rare—some, like cerium, are as abundant as copper. Their significance lies in unique magnetic, luminescent, and catalytic properties, making them critical for technologies like smartphones, electric vehicle motors, wind turbines, and defense systems (e.g., radar and missile guidance).

China has suspended rare earth exports in retaliation to President Trump’s recent tariff hikes. The trade war is starting to get real, as Beijing produces around 90% of the world’s rare earths ( 70% of global REE mining and 87% of global REE refining). There are significant strategic ramifications to this action and it raises tensions between the world’s two largest economies considerably.

The demand for rare earth elements is expected to grow 400-600% over the next few decades. Global magnet rare earth oxide consumption alone is expected to increase by 200% by the end of the decade, and more than five-fold by 2040 at $44 billion.

This wider suspension of exports comes just 9 days after China initially imposed restrictions on the export of six heavy rare earth elements. These metals and magnets come from Africa and other places, but are refined exclusively in China.

The ban applies to exports of certain rare earth minerals and magnets that are crucial for everything including cars, semiconductors, aerospace and military hardware such as jets, missiles and drones. Complicating matters, this export ban comes as supply disruptions have increased from the ongoing conflict in major supplier Myanmar.

China’s banning of rare earth exports is likely to make prices climb higher. During 2010 when China halted REE exports to Japan over a territorial dispute, the global prices spiked as much at 10x! When combining this new ban with already restricted from Myanmar, I think we are likely to see prices spike higher once again.

President Trump is drafting an executive order to enable the stockpiling of critical metals from the Pacific Ocean seabed, in an effort to counter China’s dominance of battery minerals and rare earth supply chains. Trump already has invoked emergency powers to boost the ability of the U.S. to produce critical minerals as part of a broad effort to ramp up the development of domestic natural resources to reduce reliance on imports from China and elsewhere. Part of this plan is to build metal refining facilities on military bases.

In addition to benefiting from higher rare earth prices, U.S.-based rare earth miners are likely to benefit from increased government support in the form of grants, low-interest loans and the fast-tracking of permits and licenses. This is necessary for the United States strategically to have domestic supplies of critical metals that are needed for our economy and military, especially if China’s export ban has longevity.

Given the bullish outlook above, here are 3 stocks that I think will benefit from China’s export ban on rare earth elements:

  1. MP Materials (MP) – The largest rare earth producer in the United States with the Mountain Pass Mine in California. It is the only rare-earth mining and processing facility in the United States. MP’s capabilities span the entire supply chain—from mining and processing to advanced metallization and magnet manufacturing. The company was awarded $58.5 million 48C tax credit from the government to advance the construction of America’s first fully integrated rare earth magnet manufacturing facility in Fort Worth, Texas. This comes after a $10 million grant in 2020 and $35 million grant in 2022. MP Materials delivered record production in 2024 while advancing key strategic initiatives in midstream and magnetics operations. Revenue for Q4 2024 reached $61M, up 48% year-over-year, driven by increased sales of separated rare earth products. The valuation is still high relative to peers and profit margins are weak with Q4 Non-GAAP EPS of -$0.12. The share price is up 81% year-to-date as of April 16th.
  2. Rare Element Resources (OTCQB:REEMF) – The Bear Lodge Project area is a significant mineralized district that contains one of the largest disseminated rare earth deposits in North America. This deposit contains many of the less common, more valuable rare earths and Rare Element Resources owns 100% of the Project. During the last few years, the company has been advancing their proprietary extraction/separation technology and have successfully produced commercial-grade neodymium/praseodymium (Nd/Pr) oxide in pilot-plant testing. These materials are key components to the fast-growing, permanent magnet sector. The next step will be operations of a demonstration plant, which is currently under construction in Upton, WY. Results generated from operating this plant will be used for the design and economic evaluation of a commercial rare earth processing facility. Both the Department of Energy (DOE) and the Wyoming Energy Authority (WEA) have shown their support for the Project through funding of over $26M for the demonstration plant. In March, they received a non-binding Letter of Interest from the Export-Import Bank of the United States to provide a portion of debt financing of up to $553 million for the Bear Lodge Project development’s total capital expansion. Shares are up 175% year-to-date in 2025.

  3. USA Rare Earth (USAR) – The company is building a sintered neo magnet manufacturing facility in Stillwater, OK which is currently planned to go commercial in the first half of 2026. At full capacity, this facility will be able to produce nearly 5,000 metric tons, or hundreds of millions of magnets, annually. They will serve a variety of fast-growing industries, such as defense, robotics, electric vehicles, wind power, appliances, cordless tools and even computing and semiconductors. The company’s Innovations Lab is being commissioned currently and will begin prototyping to customers this quarter (Q2, 2025). They have developed a proprietary Continuous Ion Exchange process for rare earth and critical mineral separation and have offtake agreements in place with partners in the United States and South Korea. Their unique Round Top deposit in West Texas hosts 15 of the 17 rare earth elements, including all heavy rare earth elements, as well as other high-tech metals such as gallium, hafnium, zirconium, beryllium and lithium. Long-term, the company intends to use the ore processed from this deposit in the magnet facility in Oklahoma. USA Rare Earth owns 80% of the Round Top deposit with Texas Mineral Resources Corp (OTCQB:TMRC) owning the other 20%. I covered TMRC in this 2023 article published on Seeking Alpha. USAR is up 33% year-to-date and TMRC is up 145% year-to- date in 2025. US Rare Earth also has warrants listed under USARW that are up 165% in the past 5 days alone!

Bonus Pick:

While not technically a rare earth element, Antimony has seen its price rise significantly due to supply constraints, similar to rare earth elements. China controls over 90% of the world’s antimony production, leading to concerns about supply security and price volatility. In December 2024, China banned exports of gallium, germanium, and antimony to the United States. The global supply of antimony is tightening at a time when demand is accelerating – driven by renewed defense initiatives and the global energy transition.

United States Antimony (UAMY) is the only US producer of antimony and is positioned to benefit from rising demand in defense, EV batteries, and flame retardants. They are increasing raw material supply of antimony from third parties around the world, along with staking its own claims for over 32,000 acres to reactivate U.S. historical antimony deposits and secure a fully domestic antimony supply chain. USAC operates the only significant antimony smelter in the United States and it is in a “sold out” condition. The company has proven experience in underground and open pit mining, flotation and gravimetric milling, crushing and screening, dry grinding, cyanide leaching, precious metal refining, pyro-metallurgy, and marketing. Operations include a smelter and a precious metal refinery in Montana, and a smelter and three mills in Mexico. They highlighted record revenues of $14.9 million for fiscal 2024, a 72% increase year-over-year, accompanied by a 204% growth in gross profit. The share price is up 95% year to date in 2025. Just yesterday, B. Riley Securities, Inc. raised their price target on UAMY from $3.00 to $5.00. It is currently trading at $3.45.
